hook: Which of these names is not used for Buddha?
explanation: The name Buddha today is associated with the founder of Buddhism. It means ‘awakened one’ and was used as a title by a number of religious groups in India. Gradually, it got associated with Buddhism. Buddha was born Siddhartha. He was born in the Sakya republic of Kapilavastu in present day northern Bihar. Therefore he was called Sakyamuni (the sage of the Sakya). He belonged to a kshatriya clan of the Gautama gotra and got called Gautama (Sanskrit) or Gotama (Pali). The Buddha referred to himself as Tathagata (the one who has thus gone) while the earliest Buddhist texts referred to him as Bhagavat (Lord). Vardhamana was the original name of Mahavira, who is credited with popularizing Jainism._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is from Ayuthaya in Thailand._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Why is Lumbini in Nepal a key part of Buddha’s life? Which life event of Buddha is it associated with?
explanation: Buddha’s mother was Mayadevi and his father was Suddhodana, who was an elected leader of the Sakya republic at Kapilavastu in northern Bihar with the title of Raja. His mother was on her way to her parents' home to give birth, and Buddha was born en-route, under a sal tree at Lumbini in Nepal. An Asokan inscription at the site identified it as the Buddha’s birthplace. Mayadevi died in childbirth and Buddha was brought up by his maternal aunt, Prajapati who married his father. Later, Mahaprajapati would be the first woman to become a Buddhist nun. It was at her insistence that Buddha reluctantly allowed women into the Buddhist monastic order_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ko Hon Chiu Vincent, UNESCO

hook: The Buddha performed the ‘dharma-chakra-pravartana’ at Sarnath near Kashi. What did he do?
explanation: Buddha taught the essentials of Buddhism to his first five disciples in Sarnath. He called his teaching Dhamma in the Pali language in which he preached (Dharma in Sanskrit). This event is called dharma-chakra-pravartna – turning of the wheel of law, by his followers. Why did he choose a place so far away from where he lived? It is said that the five disciples were the ones who had abandoned him earlier and had settled in Sarnath and he wanted to win them back. Kashi like now, was a magnet for holy men even then. The earliest Buddhist texts call Sarnath ‘Isipatana’ – a place where holy men landed. True to its name, it remains a centre of pilgrimage for Buddhists, Jains & Shaivites. The word Sarnath itself is related to Saranganath which means Lord of the Deer, an epithet of Lord Shiva. The Buddha also preached his first sermon at a deer park._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Varanasi Tourism

hook: This site has no connection with the life of Buddha. But it has the most ancient surviving Buddhist monuments in India. Where is it?
explanation: Sanchi stupa at Vidisha near Bhopal is considered the earliest surviving complete form of temple architecture in India. Buddhist literature including the memoirs of Chinese pilgrims hardly mention Sanchi. Many scholars believe that it relates to the story of Asoka’s marriage with a merchant’s daughter. They also believe it is the site where their son Mahendra halted during his proselytising mission. Whether the story is true or not, the earliest monuments at Sanchi date from Asoka’s time. It was in use till 12th century CE and is now a UNESCO world heritage site since 1989._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Biswarup Ganguly, Wikimedia Commons

hook: “These paintings depict colourful Buddhist legends with an exuberance and vitality that is unsurpassed in Indian art.” Which cave paintings is this citation referring to?
explanation: There are 30 caves at Ajanta – 5 are chaityas (for worship) and the rest are viharas (hostel for monks). They were carved over a 800 year period starting at the turn of first millennium CE. While the nearby Ellora caves are eclectic with Hindu, Jain and Buddhist shrines; the Ajanta caves are exclusively Buddhist. The Ellora caves are more notable for their sculpture while the paintings of Ajanta are unsurpassed. Ajanta was declared a UNESCO world heritage site in 1983._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: “More than 500 years before Oxford University was founded, this university was home to nine million books and attracted 10000 students from around the world.” Which Buddhist mahavihara is this statement referring to?
explanation: Nalanda, a Buddhist institution, was founded in 427CE by the devout Vaishnavite Gupta Emperor, Kumaragupta I. The university was continuously patronised by succeeding ruling dynasties and kings who were of different religious persuasions - the rulers of Kanauj and the Buddhist Pala kings of Bengal in particular. It combined Buddhist teaching with other subjects and contributed greatly to science, astronomy and medicine. Aryabhata, the great mathematician is said to have spent time here. Buddhist pilgrims from across the world came to Nalanda. The Dalai Lama once stated that the source of Tibetan Buddhism was Nalanda. It is said that getting admitted to the university required aspirants to go through lengthy oral interviews with the teachers. After 7 centuries of existence, it was destroyed by the forces of Bakhtiyar Khilji._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: SN Goenka has universally popularised the Buddhist way of meditation. It requires maintaining silence for 10 days continuously. What is it called?
explanation: Vipassana means ‘insight’ in Pali. It is a meditation practice where participants maintain silence for ten days continuously. Buddhist meditation is concerned with investigating the nature of experience and its effect on one’s senses. This is in keeping with the Buddhist concept of no self or conditioned arising of the self. The focus on mindfulness is different from the idea of samatha (stillness) i.e. quieting the mind through concentration on a single object which is the traditional method of meditation._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is that of the Vipassana centre in Igatpuri, Maharashtra._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Priyan Nithya, Flickr

hook: Which state in India has the maximum number of Buddhist cave temples?
explanation: There are 900-1000 rock cut cave temples In India. More than 700 of these temples are Buddhist in orientation. 80% of these cave temples are in Maharashtra, largely in the Konkan region. The soft basalt rock of Deccan plateau facilitated these temples. The Deccan area was also a Buddhist stronghold during ancient times as well as being a thriving trading route. Traders used the caves as resting & worship places enroute to busy ancient ports in Konkan. A 1st century CE book written by an Egyptian merchant mentions Chaul (Vasai), Sopara, Kalyan & Gharapuri (Elephanta) as important ports on the Konkan coast. The Kondhana (Karjat), Karle, Nanaghat & Bhimashankar were all part of important trade routes. All the places mentioned above have cave temples that served traders. The Mumbai metropolitan region was a thriving trading centre a couple of centuries before Christ._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is of Karla Caves near Lonavala. It is 2200 years old._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Kevin Standage, Wikimedia Commons

hook: Mumbai has an antiquity far older than popular imagination. Which place near Mumbai has a major Asokan rock edict?
explanation: The Rock Edict IX of Ashoka was found in Sopara, also known as Shurparaka. It was the first urban settlement to emerge in the Mumbai metropolitan area in Mauryan times and remained an important port till the Portuguese and British made Mumbai their trading base 1800 years later. It is believed to be mentioned as Ophir in the Bible. The Rock Edict is now displayed at the Mumbai Museum._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya

hook: The stories of Buddha in his former life have been and still are very popular in India. What are these stories called?
explanation: Buddha is said to have had many previous births as Bodhisattva in both human and animal forms. The stories of these Bodhisattvas illustrate the teachings of Buddha. They are among the oldest literature of Buddhism and are considered a literary classic in their own light. Later forms of Buddhism started the worship of these Bodhisattvas leading to a development of a pantheon of Gods that made Buddhism look similar to Hinduism._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is from Langza village in Spiti, Himachal Pradesh_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R Ramanujam

hook: Which day commemorates the Buddha’s birth, enlightenment and parinirvana (death)?
explanation: For much of the Buddhist world, Vesak, also called Buddha Day, Visakha Puja, or Wesak, is the most important day of the year. The name Vesak is derived from the name of the month in the Indian calendar, today called Vaisakha. The holiday falls on a full moon and can take place anytime from late April through early June on the Gregorian calendar, with Buddhist countries celebrating on different days. Vesak is also called Buddha Purnima (“full moon celebration”) or Buddha Jayanti. By the 1950s, many Asian countries celebrated International Vesak Day, and in 1999 the United Nations recognized it as an international holiday._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is from Ajanta Caves._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Which Indian State derives its name from Buddhist architecture?
explanation: Viharas were Buddhist monasteries where monks lived. Chaityas were prayer halls. Stupas were monuments that housed a relic of Buddha – his mortal remains or objects used by him. The largest and most well known viharas in India were in present day Bihar – Nalanda, Vikramshila and Odantapuri. Bihar is derived from the word Vihara, though there is no evidence of when the transition happened. Incidentally, all the three monasteries named above were destroyed by Bakhtiyar Khilji._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is from Vikramshila in Bihar._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Bihar Tourism

hook: Gurgaon is now called Gurugram (Guru’s Village). Which legendary Guru is it named after?
explanation: As per Mahabharata, King Dhritarashtra gifted the region of modern-day Gurugram to Dronacharya. It was a token of the King’s gratitude to the respected teacher for taking on the task of training the Kuru princes in the art of warfare. Dronacharya opened his school in the village, eventually settling here. Thus, the place was called ‘guru ka gaon’ meaning the teacher’s village. Over time, the name got shortened to Gurgaon, and in 2016, it was officially renamed Gurugram by the Haryana government._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.amarchitrakatha.com/mythologies/how-gurgaon-got-its-name/_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The Quint

hook: Chanakya wrote Artha Shastra. He is credited with mentoring one of the greatest emperors of India. Who is it?
explanation: Chanakya mentored Chandragupta Maurya in 4th century BC. With the wisdom and strategy of his mentor, Chandragupta Maurya destroyed the Nanda Dynasty, defeated Alexander the Great and the Greeks and established the Maurya Empire. Even though there is a dispute if Chandragupta Maurya and Alexander ever met at all, he defeated his general Selucus Nicator and also conquered territories that were under Alexander._x000D_ _x000D_ The famous 1977 Telegu film Chanakya-Chandragupta brought together doyens of South Indian cinema such as NT Rama Rao, Akkineni Nageswara Rao and Shivaji Ganeshan._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Various_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Tunes Desibantu

hook: The Pashupatas are considered as one the earliest Shaivaite sects. The Pashupatas considered their founder Lakulisha as a form of Shiva. Which famous monument in Mumbai houses a sculpture of Lakulisha?
explanation: Pashupatas are mentioned in the Mahabharata and flourished from atleast the 2nd century BCE. Their preceptor is said to be Lakulisha. He lived in Gujarat and introduced Hatha Yoga and Tantra in his philosophy._x000D_ _x000D_ As per Puranas, Shiva indicated that he would enter a dead body and incarnate himself as Lakulin (or Nakulin or Lakulisha, lakula meaning “club”). Pashupata Shaivism was popular in Gujarat, Rajasthan and the Konkan._x000D_ _x000D_ The great Shiva temple at Elephanta was excavated during the 6th century, Kalachuri period. The large sculpture panel depicting Lakulisha in a contemplative posture is situated on the left of the north entrance. The photo shown is this sculpture._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: British Library Online_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: It was the 8th century CE. Two philosophers were about to enter into a debate. The only scholar deemed worthy to be the referee was Ubhaya Bharati, a woman. The loser of the debate was her husband Mandana Mishra. Which Guru won the debate and then bestowed several honours on the loser?
explanation: Mandana Mishra was a great contemporary of Adi Shankaracharya and was initially renowned for his Mimanska philosophy. He indulged in an eight day debate with Shankara on the merits of Mimanska and Advaita. Only Mandana Mishra’s wife, Ubhaya Bharati was considered scholarly enough to act as referee. Mandana Mishra, though loser of the debate, was appointed the first head of the Sringeri math established by Shankara._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://sringeri.net/jagadgurus/sri-sureshwaracharya_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Dharmic Path, Twitter

hook: A prominent South Indian community sees the 12th century social reformer Basaveshwara or Basava as their Guru. Which community is it?
explanation: Basava, (12th century, South India), was a Hindu religious reformer, teacher, theologian, and administrator of the royal treasury of the Kalachuri-dynasty king Bijjala I (reigned 1156–67). His poetry to Shiva as “lord of the meeting rivers” earned him a place at the front rank of Kannada literature and the literature of Hindu devotion (bhakti). His life and doctrines were recorded in the Basava-purana, written by Bhima Kavi (14th century) in the Kannada language. Basava opposed caste & jati distinctions, child marriage and ill treatment of widows. In these areas, he anticipated the Hindu social reform movements of the 19th century by several centuries._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Basava_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Karnataka Tourism

hook: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu established a tradition in 15th century which has been taken forward by this contemporary movement. Which is it?
explanation: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu appeared as a devotee of Krishna in Bengal in the late fifteenth century. He introduced sankirtan, widespread congregational chanting of the Supreme Person’s names, as the most effective means by which anyone can achieve spiritual perfection. At the age of 24, he renounced the world and traveled all over India to preach his ideas. His disciples have made great contribution to restoring Vrindavan as a place of Krishna pilgrimage. The International Society for Krishna Consciousness, ISKCON, popularly known as Hare Rama Hare Krishna movement, is a continuation of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu’s sankirtan movement. Sri Prabhupada, the founder of ISCKON, is considered a representative of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu by his followers._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://iskconberkeley.us/who-is-lord-chaitanya/_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: ISKCON Desire Tree

hook: Vallabhacharya established this great temple in Rajasthan in 15th century. The Ambani family are great devotees of this temple. Which temple is this?
explanation: Vallabhacharya, ( 1479 - 1531 CE), founded the devotional sect Pushtimarg ( “way of flourishing”). Born to a Telugu Brahman family, Vallabha made Mathura his centre of his activities. He undertook several pilgrimages throughout India, propagating his doctrine of bhakti (devotion) to god Krishna. It was near Mathura, at the foot of Mount Govardhana, that Vallabha discovered the central devotional object of the sect, the deity of Krishna called Shri-Nathaji. While in flight from the Mughals, the chariot carrying the deity broke down at Nathdwara, where it was given protection by the local Rajput chief. Pushtimarg has lot of adherents in Gujarat even today_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shri-Nathaji_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Business Today

hook: Vidyaranya Swami was as influential a Guru as Chanakya. He helped establish a legendary kingdom in South India. Which is this?
explanation: Vidyaranya helped establish the Vijayanagara Empire sometime in 1336, and served as a mentor and guide to three generations of kings who ruled over it. Harihara and Bukka were two Kannada Hoysala Dynasty Army Chiefs. After the Kakatiyas were defeated by Muslim sultans, the brothers were forced to convert to Islam. Vidyaranya identified them, reconverted them back and asked them to identify a place to establish a Hindu empire._x000D_ _x000D_ Vidyaranya or Mādhavācharya, was the jagadguru of the Sringeri Sharada Peetham in the late 14th century CE. Madhavacharya is known as the author of the Sarvadarśanasaṅgraha, a compendium of different philosophical schools of Hindu philosophy._x000D_ _x000D_ The sketch is that of the brothers with Vidyaranya._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Harihara_and_Bukka_meeting_Vidyaranya.jpg_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Amar Chitra Katha

hook: Which Rajput queen is said to be the disciple of the 15th century saint Ravidas, who was born in a community considered untouchable at the time?
explanation: Ravidas, also called Raidas, a mystic and a poet, was one of the most renowned of the saints of the North Indian bhakti movement. He was born in Varanasi as a member of an untouchable leather-working caste, and his poems and songs often revolve around his low social position. His hymns are included in the Guru Granth Sahib of the Sikhs. His teachings make him a figure of pride in the contemporary Dalit movement._x000D_ _x000D_ Mirabai was married to the crown prince of the royal family of Mewar in the early 16th century CE. After the deaths of her husband and father, she became a mystic, toured the country and wrote some of the finest Bhakti poetry. Popular lore regards her as a disciple of Ravidas after she met him in Kashi. Scholars are divided, some detect the influence of Ravidas in her work while others contend that she followed the saguna ( with form) bhakti while Ravidas favored Nirguna (formless Bhakti)._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.speakingtree.in/blog/mirabais-mystical-experiences-and-about-her-guru-ravidas_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Speakingtree.in

hook: Chatrapati Shivaji had many influences in his life, but his mother is considered his Guru. Name her.
explanation: Babasaheb Purandare, the celebrated historian, was once asked what made Shivaji Maharaj special. He replied, “he had a mother like Jijabai”. Jijau as she is fondly called in Maharashtra, is as much of an inspiration as her great son._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://swarajyamag.com/magazine/mother-guru-how-jijabai-helped-her-son-achieve-his-dream-of-hindavi-swarajya_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Who was the Guru of Swami Vivekananda?
explanation: Sri Ramakrishna Paramhansa (19th century) was a yogi who translated complex spiritual concepts in an easily intelligible manner. Born in a simple Bengali rural family in 1836, he explored the Divine as defined by every path and religion. His most prominent disciple Swami Vivekananda carried on his teachings and philosophy to the world through Ramakrishna Mission. In essence, his teachings were as traditional as ancient sages, yet he remains contemporary._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.culturalindia.net/reformers/ramakrishna-paramhansa.html_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Narayana Guru inspired the reforms that have made his state a leader in social justice in the last hundred years. Which state did he belong to?
explanation: Sree Narayana Guru (1856 –1928) was a saint, philosopher, spiritual leader, and a social reformer from Kerala, India. Born in the Ezhava community, considered low on the social scale, he paved the way for social reform and change in India. So great was his influence that he attracted some of the greatest thinkers, philosophers, and fellow social reformers of his time, including Mahatma Gandhi, Rabindranath Tagore, and many others._x000D_ _x000D_ His three key teachings are: One in Kind, One in Faith, One in God, is Man; Whichever be the religion of a man, it suffices if it makes a better man; Acts that one performs for the sake of oneself should also aim for the good of others._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://sreenarayanamission.org/about-us/about-sree-narayana-guru/_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Amar Chitra Katha

hook: Which Guru’s birthplace and place of passing away are both in Pakistan?
explanation: Guru Nanak, the founder of Sikhism, was born in 1469 CE in the village Rai Bhoi di Talvandi. It is about 90 km west of Lahore. It has been renamed as Nankana Sahib in his honour. A Gurudwara was built there around 1600 CE and was renovated by Maharaja Ranjit Singh in 1819-20. Guru Nanak travelled across India and returned to Punjab by 1520. He witnessed the attacks of Babur, the first Mughal emperor. He spent his remaining years till he passed away in 1538 CE in Kartarpur, on the Indo-Pak border. Two Gurudwaras mark his stay - Dera Baba Nanak in India is where he meditated and Kartarpur Sahib in Pakistan where he breathed his last. A visa free Kartarpur corridor enables Indians to visit Kartarpur. Source: Britannica Picture Credit: The Wikimedia image is of Gurudwara Nankana Sahib.

hook: Which Guru compiled the first version of the Sikh holy book, Guru Granth Sahib?
explanation: Adi Granth (First Book) is the sacred scripture of the Sikhs. It has 6000 hymns. The first version of the book was compiled by the 5th Sikh Guru, Arjan, at Amritsar in 1604 CE. He collected the hymns of the first three Gurus, added his own compositions as also those of many other great saints of the country. In 1704 CE, the 10th and last Guru, Gobind Singh, added some more hymns._x000D_ _x000D_ The book starts with the Mul Mantra (“Basic Prayer”), which is a declaration of the nature of God as Truth, followed by the Japji (“Recital”), the most important Sikh scripture, written by Guru Nanak, the first Guru. The hymns are arranged according to the musical modes (ragas) in which they are to be sung._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Sikh Shrines in India”, GS Randhir; Britannica._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: SikhWiki image is the last page of the Granth Sahib at first floor of Harmandir Sahib in Amritsar.

hook: Which Mughal emperor executed Guru Arjan Dev?
explanation: Guru Arjan Dev (1563-1606 CE) profoundly shaped Sikhism. He compiled the Adi Granth, completed construction of the Golden Temple and stabilized the masand (tax) system of administering the community._x000D_ _x000D_ In 1605 CE, Jahangir ascended the Mughal throne. His son Khusrau rebelled against him. The Guru’s detractors spread rumours that he had blessed or supported Khusrau. He was taken before Jahāngīr, who fined him and either asked him to convert to Islam or eliminate sections of the Adi Granth deemed objectionable. Guru Arjan refused and was tortured and executed by drowning. Some say he died during torture._x000D_ _x000D_ His martyrdom made the Sikhs more militaristic and sowed the seeds of a prolonged Sikh-Mughal conflict._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Britannica_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sikhwiki image of Baba Buddha placing a tikka (a mark of distinction) on the forehead of Guru Arjan Dev, pronouncing him the fifth Guru

hook: The sixth Guru Hargobind built the supreme seat of Sikh religious authority. What is it called?
explanation: Akal Takht is adjacent to the Harmandir Saheb at the Golden Temple in Amritsar. Guru Sri Hargobind constructed the Akal Takht in 1609. The place where the Takht stands was a playground of the Guru. As Guru, he himself sat there and held a court of justice, redressing grievances of the Sikhs. Thus, the Hukumnama (order of justice) issued by the Jathedar of Akal Takht is binding on all Sikhs. Source: “Sikh Shrines in India”, GS Randhir. Picture Credit: The picture of Akal Takht is from Wikimedia Commons (Amarpreet Singh).

hook: The second Guru Angad established a tradition that is intimately associated with Gurudwaras. What is it?
explanation: Guru Angad (1504 – 52 CE) is credited with organizing the Sikh sangat. The first organized meeting of the community (sangat) took place at his family home at Taran Taran in Punjab. It is now marked by the Gurudwara Mal Akhara. Its name reflects the emphasis Guru Angad placed on physical fitness. He standardized the Gurumukhi script of the Punjabi language so that the discourses could be available to the common man in the sangat. He started the langar tradition to foster a feeling of oneness in the sangat. Source: Britannica Picture Credit: The picture of Gurudwar Mal Akhara is from Sikhwiki.

hook: Guru Amardas and Guru Ramdas, 3rd and 4th Gurus, built a structure in the Golden Temple Complex that gives the holy city of Amritsar its name. What was it?
explanation: The land that is now Amritsar was donated to the Gurus by their followers. Guru Amardas chalked out a plan to excavate a sarovar (Amrit Sarovar shortened to Amritsar). Guru Ramdas executed this plan under the supervision of Baba Budha ji. Later the fifth Guru Arjan built the Sri Harmandir Sahib (the main shrine in the Golden Temple) in the sarovar to have a focal pilgrimage centre for Sikhs – ‘ath sath tirath’. The design of Sri Harmandir sahib was personally made by Guru Arjan. Darshani Deori (main gate) and Har ki Paure (Holy Steps) are part of Sri Harmandir. Source: GoldenTempleAmritsar.org Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Which Guru stayed at the “bangla” of Raja Jai Singh of Amber, which then became the famous Bangla Sahib Gurudwara in Delhi?
explanation: The eighth Guru Harkrishan stayed at Gurudwara Bangla sahib in Delhi. Guru Harkrishan was appointed as the eight Guru by his predecessor, Guru Har Rai. His elder brother disputed the succession and approached Emperor Aurangzeb to intercede. Guru Harkrishan came to Delhi to meet the emperor and stayed here._x000D_ _x000D_ Mirza Raja Jai Singh of Amber (Jaipur) made his stay comfortable and the Guru took the opportunity to serve the people of Delhi who were facing an epidemic of cholera & small pox._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Sikh Shrines in India”, GS Randhir._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons, Nimit Nigam

hook: The seventh Guru Har Rai excommunicated his elder son. What was the reason?
explanation: Guru Har Rai (1630-1661CE) became the seventh Guru at age 14. Inspite of his age, he was an inspiring military leader following the footsteps of his grandfather, the sixth Guru Hargobind. He strove to maintain friendly relations with the Mughals. He once possibly helped cure Dara Shikoh from poisoning._x000D_ _x000D_ Aurangzeb became the Emperor after defeating Dara Shikoh. Aurangzeb summoned the Guru to explain his friendship with the defeated prince. The Guru sent his son Ram Rai to the new emperor._x000D_ Aurangzeb questioned Ram Rai about a line in the Adi Granth, deeming it against Islam. To appease the emperor, Ram Rai offered to amend the line. When the Guru came to know of this, he excommunicated his son. He then named his younger son Har Kishan as the next Guru._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Britannica_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The martyrdom of ninth Guru Tegh Bahadur is told by the Gurudwaras of Sisganj and Rakabganj. What do Sis and Rakab refer to?
explanation: Guru Tegh Bahadur was executed on the orders of Emperor Aurangzeb in 1675. He paid the price for espousing the cause of the freedom of worship of Kashmiri Pandits. He was executed at Chandni Chowk in Delhi at a place marked by Gurudwara Sis Ganj, ‘sis’ means head. His followers escaped with his severed head to Anandpur Sahib in Punjab under great danger._x000D_ _x000D_ His headless body was secretly carried by Lakhi Shah and his son to their house in Delhi to avoid any desecration. They burned down their house to cremate the Guru. Gurudwara Rakabganj (‘rakab’ colloquially means body and in Persian it refers to stirrup of a horse) stands at this spot, very close to Parliament house._x000D_ _x000D_ The Sikh General Baghel Singh captured Delhi in 1783CE and forced the weak Emperor Shah Alam to allow him to build small shrines at the two locations. These were expanded later in the 20th cent CE after multiple legal disputes with local Muslims were settled._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: SikhWiki_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The picture of Gurudwara Sisganj from Flickr (dan Be)

hook: Which Guru's compositions are called Dasam Granth?
explanation: The tenth Guru Gobind Singh was a saint, leader, soldier & scholar/poet – a rare combination. He wrote many books in Punjabi, Persian & Hindi. Guru's literary works were of the highest philosophical and poetical quality. His poetry shows a mastery of the musical metre._x000D_ _x000D_ Dasam Granth is the name given to the compositions of Guru Gobind Singh. These compositions were lost when the Guru vacated Anandgarh. They were later gathered and reproduced by his companion and disciple Bhai Mani Singh who also filled in some of the gaps._x000D_ _x000D_ Dasam Granth includes Jap (similar to Vishnu Sahasranama), Akal Ustat, Gyan Prabodh and Bachittar Natak. It is semi autobiographical with ruminations on Guru's past lives._x000D_ _x000D_ Dasam Granth also delves into avatars of Vishnu, Brahma, Rudra and Chandi Charitra (from Markandeya Purana). Some question the authenticity of authorship of these parts._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Gurudwara Gazette, SGPC_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Dasam Granth at British Library, Wikimedia Commons

hook: Mahavira is a Conqueror or a Jina. Whom did he conquer?
explanation: Jina is known as the one who conquers inner enemies like anger, greed, passion, ego etc. Ancient Indian, Greek and Chinese traditions advocated that it was braver for a man to conquer his desires than his enemies and that the hardest victory is over the self. The followers of the Jina are called Jains. The picture is of Kundalpur near Nalanda in Bihar. It is believed to be the birthplace of Mahavira though two other sites also have the same claim. Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Tirthankaras are synonymous with Jainism. They are spiritual teachers of Dharma. How many Tirthankaras does the Jain tradition record?
explanation: Mahavira was the 24th and final Tirthankara of Jainism. He is tentatively dated to have been born in 599 BCE and achieved nirvana in 527CE, about 50 years before the birth of Buddha. The 23rd Tirthankara, Parsvanatha is supposed to have lived 250 years before Mahavira. The first Tirthankara, Rsabhanatha, Rishabhanatha or Vrsabha finds repeated mention in the Rig Veda. Some scholars suggest that the meditative posture depicted in Harappan seals is very similar to that practiced by Jain ascetics. The picture is that of Tirthankaras from Karkala in Karnataka. Source: “Jaina Shrines in India,” OP Tandon Britannica.com Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: What does the word ‘Tirthankara’ mean?
explanation: Tirthankara means a saviour who has succeeded in fording or crossing over life’s streams of rebirths. He has made a path for others to follow. You may note that Hindus also call their sacred places as Tirthas. The word Tirtha (pilgrimage) is used in a similar sense. Visiting a Tirtha helps in winning over the cycle of birth and death. The picture is that of Parsvanatha, the 23rd Tirthankara. Source: “India : A Sacred Geography,” Diana Eck Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Bahubali deity in Karnataka (Sravanabelagola near Hassan) is described as a ‘sermon in stone.’ A famous emperor renounced his kingdom and retired to this area until his death. Who is it?
explanation: Chandragupta founded the Mauryan empire in the wake of Alexander’s invasion in late 4th century BCE, under the guidance of Chanakya / Kautilya. Chandragupta is ascribed with strong leanings towards Jainism. Megasthenes refers to the presence of Sramanas (Jain ascetics) at his court. He is said to have renounced his throne and accompanied the Jain saint Bhadrabahu to South India during a famine. Legend suggests that he died by practicing a fast._x000D_ _x000D_ A group of Jain temples called Chandragupta basadi at Chandragiri hill in Sravanabelagola, Karnataka commemorate his association with the place._x000D_ _x000D_ The Bahubali, (also known as Gomatesvara) deity at Sravanabelagola in Karnataka is 57 feet high. It is considered as the tallest monolith deity in the world. It is more than a 1000 years old. Every 12 years, in one of the greatest Jain rites, the entire deity is bathed in curd, milk and ghee._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Jaina Shrines in India,” OP Tandon_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: The first epigraphic evidence of the word Bharatvarsha is in the inscriptions of the Jain king Kharavela. It is within modern city limits now. In which city is this?
explanation: Hathigumpha (Elephant Cave) is in Udaygiri hills in Bhubaneswar, Odisha. The cave has an inscription that describes the biography of one of the most powerful rulers of India – King Kharavela of the Mahamegavahana family. He was a great patron of Jainism and lived in early 2nd century BCE. He avenged the historical defeat of Kalinga at the hands of Magadhan kings, notably Ashoka. He brought back the image of Jina that had been taken from Kalinga to Magadha. The 10th line of the inscription includes the phrase “Bharadavasa pathanam mahijayanam” in Prakrit. Source: “The Hathigumpha Inscription of Kaharavela and The Bhabhru Edict of Asoka,” Shashi Kant Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Anekantavada is a central tenet of Jainism. We all know this tenet through a famous story, though we may not know its links with Jainism. What is this story?
explanation: Translated, Anekantavada literally means “no-one-perspective-ism”; in other words, the multiplicity and relativity of views. This means the arguments espoused by the various participants in a debate all hold some validity. A complete understanding of any truth requires the consideration and acceptance of a variety of viewpoints. The story of five blind men and an elephant is mostly reckoned to be a Jain contribution. Ahimsa (non violence) and Anekantavada (many pointedness) are said to be the two basic pillars of Jain philosophical tradition. Source: https://pluralism.org/anekantavada-the-relativity-of-views Picture Credit:

hook: The first known Jain sculptures date back to the early part of 1st century CE. They were discovered at Kankali tila. This famous archeological site is in a sacred city. Which city is this?
explanation: The Kankali Tila is in Mathura. It is the site of archeological remains of Jain temples, stupas and sculptures. The historian Meenakshi Jain writes, “The ritual culture of images developed almost simultaneously in all three traditions of Buddhism, Jainism and Hinduism, in the city of Mathura.” The earliest image from Mathura is said to be the ‘Isapur Buddha’ from 15 CE. Before this innovation at Mathura, Gods were depicted aniconically – Buddha by a chakra, Mahavira by tri-ratna, Vishnu & Shiva by saligrama & linga. Deities and human figures were made but they represented Yakshas & Nagas and not Gods. Source: “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura,” Meenakshi Jain. Picture Credit: Biswarup Ganguly, Wikimedia Commons

hook: This temple complex in Gujarat has hundreds of Jain temples built on a hill. Which is this town?
explanation: Shatrunjaya Hill is one of the holiest pilgrimage sites of Jains. It is in Palitana, 51 km from Bhavnagar in Gujarat. The sacred hill contains hundreds of shrines. It was sanctified when Rishabanatha, the first Tirthankara of Jainism, gave his first sermon on the hilltop. The temples were built over 900 years starting from 11th century CE. Kumarpal Solanki, a great Jain patron, built the first temples on this site. The temples were destroyed by Turkish Muslim invaders in 1311 AD. The saint Jinaprabhasuri, who was then 50 years old, presided over the temples, and later restored them to their glory. Source: https://www.gujarattourism.com/saurashtra/bhavnagar/palitana-and-shatrunjaya-hill.html Picture Credit: trawell.in

hook: The Dilwara group of temples are amongst the most well recognised Jain temples of India. Where are these?
explanation: The five Jain temples made of marble are at Dilwara near Mount Abu in Rajasthan. They use the marble stones from Makrana as did the Taj Mahal. They were built between the 11th and 15th century by different rulers. They are the best specimens of the Maru-Gurjara branch of North Indian Nagara temple architecture attributed to the Solanki rulers of Gujarat. Profusely carved pillars, ceilings and flying arches in large, open pillared halls, are characteristics of this style. Ranakpur, also in Rajasthan has similiar architecture and intricacy. Source: “The International Jaina Style,” Julia A B Hegewald Picture Credit: Luna Vasahi

hook: This well known site in India has colossal rock cut sculptures of Tirthankaras. Where is this site?
explanation: The Gwalior rock sculptures are unique for their colossal size and number. They are spread around the Gwalior fort in five groups and are several dozen in number. The rock cut sculptures are upto 57 feet tall. They were excavated in the 15th century in the reign of Tomara Rajas. Very soon after that, they were mutilated by Babur who has recorded the destruction in his own words._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: "Four Reports 1862-65," Alexander Cunningham_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: This Jain temple is in a site that is well known for its group of temples. Where is this temple?
explanation: This is the Parsvanath temple in Khajuraho which also has a Jain temple complex. There are temples for Parsvanath, Adinath and Shantinath. The Parsvanath temple is one among the largest Jain temples. The temple architecture is similar to that of the other temples in Khajuraho, especially the Lakshmana Temple. Its sculptures show a predominance of Vaishnava themes, with sculptures of Parasurama, Balarama with Revati, Rama, Sita and Hanuman. Source: "Khajuraho," Krishna Deva, ASI Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: This Jain temple site is also the site of one of the first surviving structural temples of Hinduism. Where is this site?
explanation: Deogarh in Uttar Pradesh has the largest collection of Jaina images numbering around 2000 and numerous Jain temples. Deogarh is more than the sum of its published monuments.. and is the greatest collection of Jaina images anywhere in India. The Dashavatara temple in Deogarh is also considered as one of the earliest surviving Hindu temples. Source: "Jainism: A Pictorial Guide to the Religion of Non-violence," Kurt Titze, Klaus Bruhn Picture Credit: Ed Senter, Flickr

hook: How old is the evidence of Mango in India?
explanation: The genome of Mangifera Indica, mango, has been detected 60 million years ago. Mango leaf fossils (Paleocene era) have been recovered near Damalgiri, West Garo Hills, Meghalaya. This was even before joining of the Indian and Asian continental plates._x000D_ _x000D_ Wild species of genus Mangifera are distributed throughout South and South East Asia. Cultivation started much later. The consensus among historians is that cultivation of mangoes started in the north east India about 4000 years ago._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: AI generated mango leaf fossil, GenCraft

hook: Mango is in our ancient sculptures too. Where will you find the earliest sculptural evidence of mango in India?
explanation: Buddha had committed to perform a miracle under a mango tree in Sravasti. To make it impossible for Buddha to perform the miracle, his opponents uprooted all mango trees. While Buddha was on his begging rounds, the King’s gardener presented Buddha with a ripe mango. Buddha ate the fruit, planted the seed and washed his hands over the seed. Instantly the seed grew upto a full mango tree. Buddha would go on to perform another miracle at the mango tree. Sanchi has a sculpture on the Mango Miracle of Buddha at Sravasti. The gateways of the great stupa at Sanchi (1st century CE) and the railings at Bharhut stupa (2nd century CE) have 40 representations of plants including the mango. Sanchi stupa sculptures show among other representations an elephant heading towards a mango tree laden with fruits and monkeys on a mango tree looking for the fruit. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The earliest literary evidence of mango in India is in which of these texts?
explanation: Valmiki Ramayana has many references to mango. For e.g., Balakand describes the city of Ayodhya, “(it) accommodates groups of danseuses and theatrical personnel, and is surrounded everywhere with the gardens and brakes of mango trees, and her wide fort wall is like her cincture ornament”. Later, there are profuse references in Sanskrit dramas and Puranas. There is also a theory that there must be a reference in Vedas to mangoes, since mango leaves are used in homams. But historians don’t concur._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Bishnu Sarangi, Pixabay

hook: Which Indian language is the English word ‘Mango’ borrowed from?
explanation: The English word mango originated from Tamil “mangai” and Malayalam “manga.” Cashew, same as mango family, is called Kashumaanga in Malayalam! The Malayan word for mango is manga, same as Tamil. After its domestication in India more than 4000 years ago, traders, travelers and rulers have taken the mango across the globe. During 4-5th centuries BCE the Buddhist monks took mango to Malaya Peninsula and East Asia. Chinese traveler Hiuen T’sang returned from India to China with the mango in the 7th century CE. Further, in the 10th century CE the Persians carried it to East Africa. During 16th century CE the Portuguese have taken it to West Africa & Brazil and thence to West Indies where the British picked it up._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Rajesh India, Flickr

hook: Mango is the national fruit of India and Pakistan. Mango Tree is the national tree of Bangladesh. It is the national fruit of one more country. Which is this?
explanation: The genus Mangifera contains 41 valid species out of which the maximum number are located in the Malaya region which is generally considered as the birth place of the wild growing mango. The species are spread out from India to the Phillipines and the mango is considered important in this belt. But the only cultivated species of mango is Mangifera indica, the cultivation of which first started 4000 years ago in NE India from where monks and traders took them to SE Asia. The picture is a mango orchard from the Guimaras province of Philippines. Like others, it claims to have the sweetest mango in the world. Picture Credit: tsinelaslife.com

hook: Alphonso mangoes are expensive and are a popular export. They are related to a colonial power. Which power is it?
explanation: Alfonso is named after its Portuguese cultivator in India, Nicolau Alfonso. Little else is known of the creator._x000D_ _x000D_ The famous varieties over which Indians squabble so much, started appearing only after the Portuguese introduced grafting. This technique was then patronized by Mughal rulers. Nicola Manucci, an Italian traveler in Aurangzeb’s court, notes that Portuguese created many varieties which carried the names of individual cultivators. These mangoes were a favourite of Emperor Shahjahan, who had a dedicated supply from Mazgaon to Delhi. Today, it is grown in the Konkan region and is the one of the prized exports from India._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Joquim, pixabay

hook: Sher Shah celebrated his victory over Humayun with this mango variety. Which is it?
explanation: Sher Shah defeated Humayun at Chausa near Buxar in Bihar in 1539 and at Kannauj in Western UP in 1540. Sher Shah named a mango variety Chausa, to mark his victory. Chausa is very popular in the north of India. With these victories, Sher Shah drove Humayun out of India. Humayun regained his throne in 1556CE after the second battle of Panipat where the successors of Sher Shah were defeated. Picture Credit: Indian Institute of Horticultural Research

hook: Darbhanga in Bihar had a plantation of one lakh mangoes called Lakhi-bagh. Which emperor planted this?
explanation: The Jesuit delegation to Akbar in 1579 is said to have introduced the idea of grafting mangoes to the emperor. He is said to have planted one lakh trees in Darbhanga, Bihar to introduce this technique on a large scale. This led to the creation of many mango varieties. While the story is not in Ain-i-Akbari, it is accepted by historians as true. The Mughals loved mangoes, introducing recipes like the Aaam Panna, Aaam ki Lauz and Aam ka Meetha Pulao. Nurjehan is said to have created wines mixed with mangoes. Picture Credit: AI generated image of one lakh mango trees, GenCraft

hook: Some folk stories suggest that a famous Ramayana character introduced the mango to India. Who was he?
explanation: When Hanuman reached Lanka in search of Sita, he is supposed to have encountered an Aamra – Kanan (Mango grove). After having done justice to the fruits, he is said to have thrown the seeds across the sea to India where they took root. Hanuman is also known to have taken a flight towards the Sun mistaking it for a mango. A story of contest between Ganesha and Karthikeya has the prize as a golden mango. We know that the mango is indigenous to India but the story illustrates how most things in India have been linked to the itihasa-purana tradition. Picture Credit: Salangpur Hanumanji, Twitter

hook: Ekamrakshetra is an earlier name of this temple city. It describes a single mango tree. Which city is this?
explanation: The original name of Bhubaneswar is Ekamra-kshetra because the presiding deity, Lingaraj was originally under a mango tree. The Brahma Purana states that, “In Utkala or Orissa there is the kshetra of Lord Shiva, Krittivasas. It removes all sins, and such places are difficult to come across. It has a crore of Siva Lingas and is equal in merit to Banares. It is known as Ekamra and has eight principal tirthas.”_x000D_ _x000D_ Kancheepuram in Tamil Nadu is also called Emkarakshetra and houses the famous Ekambaranathar Temple._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Mango is a central piece of a political assassination in the Indian subcontinent. Who was killed in this incident?
explanation: Zia-ul-Haq was an army general and martial law president of Pakistan. He died in a plane crash in 1988, at the height of his power. Several of his senior colleagues and the US Ambassador to Pakistan also died with him. A box of mangoes had been gifted to him just before the flight. Popular belief is that a bomb was hidden among the mangoes. There is no official admission that he was assassinated, leave alone the role of mangoes._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: AI generated image of an exploding mango, GenCraft

hook: India has a dozen GI (Geographical Identification) tagged mango varieties. Which State has got the maximum?
explanation: Malda alone (West Bengal) has three GI tagged mango varieties, Laxman Bhog, Himsagar and Fazil. Uttar Pradesh and Maharashtra have two each. Kerala, Karnataka, Gujarat, Bihar and Andhra have one each. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Your friend knocks on your door. He is wishing you a happy new year. He is carrying Ghila Pitha or Til Pitha, the traditional new year sweet of his State. Which State is he likely to be from?
explanation: It has to be Assam. Ghila Pitha is a deep fried delicacy. It is made of rice flour, traditionally the Assamese Bora variety. Sooji and gur are the other ingredients. It is the snack made during Magh Bihu of Assam. It is also served as a regular household tea time snack. Ring up your assamese friend and ask him to come._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Archanas Kitchen

hook: Odisha celebrates its new year as Pana Sankranti. It derives its name from the cuisine of the day, Pana. What is Pana?_x000D_ _x000D_ A trademark observance of Odisha new year is letting water drop from a pot onto a Tulsi plant.
explanation: Odisha celebrates its new year on April 14 or 15, following the solar calendar. Pana is made during the festival. It is a drink made with bel fruit pulp (wood apple) or sweet mango. Milk or yoghurt and coconut water are the other ingredients. Open the fridge and look for Pana now._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Kamalakanta777, Wikimedia Commons

hook: This State too follows solar calendar and celebrates new year on April 14 or 15. Chaler Payesh is one of the sweets served. Which State is it most likely to be?
explanation: The Bengali new year is called Poila Baisakh. The name means the first day (Poila/ Pahela) of the month Vaisakh. The Bengali calendar is a mix of the Hindu solar and Muslim Hejiri calendar and is supposed to have been started in 1584 during Akbar’s reign. Bengali businessmen and shopkeepers open new account books, ‘haalkhaata’ on this day and take the blessings of deities for the new year. No Bengali festival is complete without food and there is an enormous variety of dishes served on Poila Baisakh. The Chaler Payesh is the equivalent of the North Indian rice kheer and the South Indian payasam and is a popular dessert. Want to rush to Kolkata to sample this?_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Slurrp.com

hook: Which State serves this menu on its astronomical new year?
explanation: Vishu Sadhya, like every Sadhya of Kerala is an elaborate and exquisite meal. This Sadhya has 24 items and is being advertised for pre order in Qatar._x000D_ _x000D_ Vishu is the astronomical new year of Kerala. The first day of Malayalam calendar is called Chingam and falls in August and is also observed as a new year._x000D_ _x000D_ Start the Vishu feast now, it is so elaborate, it will be Chingam by the time you finish it.

hook: Which State makes Puran Poli as the signature dish for its new year?
explanation: Puran Poli is the signature sweet on Gudi Padwa, the Maharashtrian New Year. Gudi Padwa follows the lunar calendar and occurs in March or April. Puran Poli is roti stuffed with sweet. The stuffing or Puran is made of Chana dal and jaggery. The roti or Poli is made of a mix of wheat and maida. It is popular in entire south India and is called Boli or Poli or Bobbatlu or Obbattu or Holige in various South Indian States. Ready to roll your poli?_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Usha Nagle, Wikimedia Commons

hook: Ugadi Pachidi is a new year dish in Andhra, Telangana, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. It has a specific number of ingredients to signal the mix of experiences in life. How many are there?
explanation: This is called Ugadi Pachidi (Telanagana and Andhra), Bevu Bella (Karnataka) and Mangai Pachidi (Tamil Nadu). The key ingredients are tamarind, mango, neem, jaggery, chilli and salt representing the six tastes. Each taste also refers to a particular emotion or experience of life._x000D_ _x000D_ The pachidi signals the variety of experiences and emotions that each new year will inevitably bring. Is your sixth sense telling you that someone is making Ugadi Pachidi around the corner? Maybe!_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sailaja, Flickr

hook: Tehar is made by Kashmiri pandits for their new year. What is likely to be an ingredient of Tehar?
explanation: Turmeric._x000D_ _x000D_ Kashmiri Hindus consider Navreh, their new year, to be as sacred and holy as Maha Shivaratri. Navreh customs are remarkably similar to Vishu of Kerala that is on the other corner of India._x000D_ _x000D_ Tehar or turmeric rice made with ghee is a special preparation for the new year. It is also offered as a symbol of gratitude when anyone recovers from an illness or any unfortunate incident. Kashmiri pandits also rinse walnuts in running river water as a custom during the new year. Yes, the humble rice is a constant feature of many new year recipes, from Kerala Sadhya to Chaler Payesh to Vishu Sadhya._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Autormata.com

hook: Who is most likely to make Tahiree or Guji Deg, a sweet rice, on their new year?
explanation: Guji Deg or Tahiree is a sweet rice dish. This is offered to Jhulelal by Sindhis on their new year day, which is called Cheti Chand. The rice is made with ghee, cardamom, saffron, sugar and milk. Yes, the humble rice custom was even prevalent across the border._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Archanas Kitchen

hook: ‘Pillaiyar’ is one of the most ancient names of Ganesha. In which state is it most commonly used?
explanation: Pillaiyar is the name of Ganesha in Tamil. ‘Pille’ means child and Pillaiyar means ‘noble child’ though some scholars believe the original meaning to be ‘young of an elephant’. Hiuen Tsang (7th cent CE) in his travels mentions the elephant deity called ‘Pillushara’._x000D_ _x000D_ It seems that Pillaiyar is derived from Pilushara for when words were borrowed into Tamil, the Sanskrit ‘sa or sha’ was sometimes changed into ‘ya’. The ancient name of Ganesha thus survives in south India where many of the Hindu traditions still continue in an unadulterated form._x000D_ _x000D_ The image shows a Ganesha from Gardez, near Kabul. This is attributed to the Turk Shahi kings of Afghanistan, 6th or 8th Century CE._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: MK Dhavalikar, “Origin of Ganesa’, BORI._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: ISKapoor, Wikimedia Images

hook: The first known evidence of elephant worship is outside India, in a place called Kapisa. In which modern country is it?
explanation: Kapisa is a province of Afghanistan centred around the modern city of Bagram. There is a 2nd cent BCE Indo Greek coin with an elephant deity described as “kapise nagara devata.” Kapisa can mean either ‘endowed with elephants’ or ‘monkey coloured’ (brown). Many cities of that area have elephant based names – Kapisa (Bagram), Pushkaravati (Peshawar), Hastinapur, Varanavat etc. Some names of Ganapati describe his brown colour - Kapil (yellowish brown) and Haridra (golden brown). Greek historians describe how Alexander worshipped an elephant at Taxila. They say that the north west of India was populated by a tribe of elephant worshippers called Hastikas. Alexander issued a coin with an elephant shaped crown on his head, a practice later adopted by many Indo Greek rulers. Historians conjecture that after defeating the Hastikas, the Greeks adopted their deity to pacify them. Source: MK Dhavalikar, “Origin of Ganesa’, BORI. Picture Credit: New York Public Library Digital Collection

hook: Gana-esha, Gana-pati, Gana-adhyaksha etc. All these names refer to Ganesha as ‘Lord of Gana.’ What does gana mean?
explanation: Shiva’s companions or ‘ganas’ are short hardy spirits, a sort of Yakshas, mainly malevolent. It is symbolic that Shiva is the God of all, including the different and the wretched. After giving back Ganesha His life by installing an elephant head, Shiva makes Ganesha the leader of His ganas, as per the Puranas. Ganapati is also mentioned in the Rig Veda, but refers to Brihaspati and Indra. Gana also mean people – Republic of India is Bharat Gana-rajya. Thus Gana- pati, Gana-esha etc. came to mean Lord of the people or Raja. Ganpati pandals in Mumbai invariably call Him Raja of the locality where the pandal is made e.g. Andheri cha Raja. The Wikimedia image is of a relief at Ellora depicting the marriage of Shiva & Parvati. The ganas are in the background. Source: Ganesha, the Auspicious, the Beginning. Shakuntahla Jagannathan and Nanditha Krishna Picture Credit: Britannica.com

hook: Vakratunda refers to a common feature of Ganesha. Which is it?
explanation: There are many names of ganesh that describe his elephant features. Gajavakra (elephant trunk), Gajakarna (elephant ears), Gajanan, Hastimukha & Gajananeti (elephant face), Gajavaktra (elephant mouth), Shoorpakarna & Lamabakarna (large ears), Lambodra (big belly). The image shows an elephant deity on the coin of Hermaeus, and Indo Greek ruler (1st-2nd cent CE). Notice the prominent curved trunk._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Glory of Ganesha, Chinmaya Mission_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: MK Dhavalikar, “Origin of Ganesa”, BORI

hook: Ganesha was also a war prize. “Vatapi Ganaptim Bhaje” is a very popular song in Carnatic music. It refers to the victor of a battle at Vatapi carrying the Ganesha deity back to their home State. Where is Vatapi?
explanation: Vatapi refers to Badami, in the Bijapur (now Viyayapura) district of Karnataka. Tradition holds that in the 7th Century CE, the Pallava kings brought back the deity from Vatapi as a booty, after their victory over Chalukyas. Pallavas and Chalukyas had been bitter rivals. The deity was brought to the home town of Paranjyoti, the Pallava general who later on became a Saivite Saint._x000D_ _x000D_ The song is in Sanskrit. It was composed in the 18th/19th century CE by Muttuswami Dikshitar, one of the trinities of Carnatic Music. The song is very popular and is rendered as the opening song in most Carnatic music concerts._x000D_ _x000D_ The song reflects the universality of Ganesha. It straddles several centuries of history; is composed in Sanskrit in the heart of Tamil culture; and links the history of northern Karnataka with Tamil Nadu._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Catlin, Amy. "Vātāpi Gaṇapatim": Sculptural, Poetic, and Musical Texts in the a Hymn to Gaṇeśa"._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Dinamalar

hook: ‘Modakpriya mud mangal data, vidya varidhi buddhi vidhata.’ This is from Ganesh Vandana, by a poet, who is a household name, for another epic composition. Who is this poet?
explanation: Tulsidas wrote Ramcharitmanas which adorns all devout North Indian homes. He also wrote Ganpati Vandana which describes Ganesha as Modakpriya - lover of modak._x000D_ _x000D_ “Gaiye Ganapati jagvandan sankar suwan bhawani ke nandan,_x000D_ Siddhi sadan gajbadan vinayak kripasindhu sundar sab laayak ,_x000D_ Modak priya mud mangal data vidya varidhi buddhi vidhata ,_x000D_ Maangat tulsidas kar jore basahu ram siya manas more”._x000D_ _x000D_ A North Indian saint celebrating a quintessential Maharashtrian delicacy 500 years ago!_x000D_ _x000D_ The Picture is of a single Modak weighing 72kgs offered to the Vinayaka in Madurai Meenakshi temple_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Indian Express

hook: The oldest name of Ganesha is mentioned in the Rig Veda. What could this name be?
explanation: The name Ganapati is mentioned in Rig Veda. However it does not refer to Ganesha. It is mentioned as an attribute of Brihaspati, the lord of wisdom who heads a group of mantras. Indra and Rudra are also referred as Ganapati in Vedic literature._x000D_ _x000D_ It is conjectured that it simply means a leader. Other old names include Ganesha (as Ganeshwara, Ganesha is not found in early Sanskrit literature) and Vinayaka, both of whom are present in Sutra literature and Mahabharata. Both have slightly malevolent characteristics in early references._x000D_ _x000D_ Scholars thus think that Ganesha & Vinayaka were Yaksha Gods and the early sculptures from Mathura from the 2nd-3rd cent CE, indicate that._x000D_ _x000D_ The image shows the gigantic Ganapati of Unakoti, considered timeless._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: AK Narain, “Ganesha: The Idea & The Icon”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: The Ashtavinayak circuit is a pilgrimage to eight popular Ganesha temples very close to each other. In which State will you find this pilgrimage?
explanation: Popularly called Astavinayak temples, the group of eight Ganapati temples are around the Pune and Konkan region. The temples are in Morgaon, Mahad, Pali, Lenyadri, Ranjangaon, Siddhatek, Theur and Ozar. Astavinayak is a popular pilgrimage that many pilgrims cover in two or three days._x000D_ _x000D_ The deities are monolith and are also Swayambhu, self formed and not man made._x000D_ _x000D_ It is a commonly held belief that if a pilgrim visits any one of the 8 temples, they must visit the others as well to receive the full benefit of the pilgrimage._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Glory of Ganesha. Chinmaya Mission._x000D_ _x000D_ Temples and Legends of Maharashtra. Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan

hook: Ganesha is most popular in Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu. However, this famous pilgrimage city in North India has a group of 56 Vinayaka deities, many lost in time. Which is this city?
explanation: The Vinayakas are arranged eight each (representing the directions) in seven concentric circles around the Vishwanath temple. The Kasi Khanda of Skanda Purana provides a description of the 56 Vinayakas._x000D_ _x000D_ Kashi was ruled by King Divodasa. After Shiva married Parvati, he wished to settle in Kashi and sent Vinayaka to get Divodasa to vacate the city. Vinayaka, by a trick, caused Divodasa to invite a curse by which the city was emptied. Thereafter Shiva and Parvati could settle down in Kashi. Vinayaka established himself in 56 forms in the city to protect it._x000D_ _x000D_ Although the reference to 56 Vinayakas is well established in Brahmanda Purana, the physical location of the 56 Vinayakas is uncertain._x000D_ _x000D_ Source and Picture Credit: Isabelle Bermijn. An Investigation Into The Fifty-Six Vinayakas In Banaras And Their Origins

hook: In which country outside India is this form of Ganesha, as an embracing couple, worshipped popularly?
explanation: Ganesha worship is very popular in Japan and spread through Buddhism. Sōshin Kangiten is the most enigmatic and elusive Japanese version of Gaṇeśa. Ganesha represents Dual God of Boundless Bliss. The Japanese Dual Kangiten is an elephant-headed deity couple in embrace. It represents the unity of contrasting genders and opposites. Avalokiteśvara (a Bodhisatva) is believed to have seduced and calmed down Vinayaka resulting in the harmonious union. Source: Lode Rosseels, Gaṇeśa’s Underbelly, Universiteit Gent Picture Credit: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna

hook: Holi has references in India's ancient manuscripts like Kamasutra and Mimansa. However, the first full-scale description of the festivities appeared in the 7th Century Sanskrit play Ratnavali. Do you know which great emperor scholar wrote it?
explanation: Emperor Harshavardhana of Thanesar wrote three plays under the title Sri Harsha. Ratnavali was written to be performed during the spring festivities ‘Vasantotsava.’ His description of Holi resembles the present-day celebration of Holi. He writes, “Citizens are dancing at the touch of water from syringes. They are voluntarily seized by lovely women now under the exhilaration of wine. The roads all around are reverberating with wild music and songs sung by enthusiastic city dwellers, and all the quarters are rendered yellowish red with a mass of fragrant powder scattered about”._x000D_ _x000D_ Ratnavali is not the only ancient manuscript to mention Vasantotsava or Holi. The term Holaka is used in Mimamsa Sutra. Kathakagrhya refers to the term Rata Holake and mentions that Hola is a special rite performed for women where Raka or Full Moon is the deity. These are a few centuries before Christ._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: We asked Dall E, the artificial intelligence engine to simulate a picture of Ratnavali celebrations.

hook: One feature of today’s Holi celebration finds a mention in the ancient Kamasutra. ‘Srngakrida,’ is believed to be one of the integral parts of Holi. Do you know what it refers to?
explanation: Kamasutra is the first literary work that mentions Holi (Holaka) as a spring festival. The commentary of Jayamangala on Kamasutra describes the festival as a sprinkling of each other with coloured water on the Phalguna Full Moon (the day of Holi). The term SrngaKrida is used to describe this. Srnga means a water gun or syringe, and SrngaKrida involved throwing colored water on people with the help of Picchkari or Syringe._x000D_ _x000D_ Similarly, SechanaKride, is a Holi-like game depicted with a sculpture from the 12th-century Belur Chennakesava Temple. This sculpture miniature depicts a Madhanika playing the game with her attendants, filling pichkari and the attendants (in miniature) handing out water vessels._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Dharmashastras - Volume 5, P.V. Kane_x000D_ _x000D_ Vasantosava, Spring Festivals of India, Leona Anderson_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Holi as a festival commands great importance in our popular culture. Can you name the first movie in which a Holi celebration was featured?
explanation: Aurat (1940), Comrades (1939) and Ghar Ki Laxmi (1031) all feature a Holi song. Aurat is considered as the first film with a Holi celebration on screen. Comrades, a 1939 film features a traditional Holi song, "mope daar gayo saari rang ki gagar." This in turn is the same song that featured in the earlier film Ghar ki Laxmi (1931) whose records are lost._x000D_ _x000D_ Aurat was a 1940 film directed by Mehboob Khan. The film has the Holi celebration song "Jamuna Tath ShyamKhelen Holi." The music for the film was composed by Anil Biswas (as was for Comrades). This song had been first recorded in audio in 1910. The movie describes the travails of a young mother in an Indian village whose husband has abandoned her. If that plot sounds familiar to you, it is. Aurat was remade as Mother India by Mehboob in 1953. Interestingly, Mother India, too, featured a Holi celebration that is now legendary, "Holi Aayi Re Kanhai."_x000D_ _x000D_ If you know more about the Comrades song and if it featured a Holi celebration, do let us know._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: bollywood.blogspot.com, Ashish Dafria, atulsongaday.me_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: YouTube

hook: This Gurudwara is the venue of a major annual celebration called Hola Mohallah. This is usually held on the day after Holi. Where is this Gurudwara?
explanation: The name Hola is the masculine form of the feminine-sounding Holi. The custom originated in the time of Guru Gobind Singh when he held the first march at Anandpur in 1701. Unlike the traditional Holi, the Guru made it an occasion for the Sikhs to demonstrate their martial skills in simulated battles. His followers later assembled on this day to celebrate by engaging in sieges, weapon training, mock battles, and military exercises. Hola Mohallah became an annual tournament held in the open ground near Holgarh Fort. The fort itself is named after the Holi celebrations. Sikhs in large numbers assemble at Anandpur Sahib. They take out an impressive and colourful procession. The Nihangs, in their traditional panoply, form the vanguard. They display skills in the use of arms, horsemanship, and tent pegging. Source: The Encylopaedia of Sikhism, Volume II, Harbans Singh. Sikhism, William Owen Punjab Disctrit Gazetteer, Rupnagar, 1987. Picture Credit: Hsingh43, Wikimedia Commons

hook: In this State of India, Holi has a distinct religious dimension. Krishna is worshipped in a swing during Holi, and the festival is called Dolyatra. Where is this form of celebration practiced?
explanation: In Bengal, Assam and Orissa, the festival holds great significance for Krishna Bhakts. Purnima, the full moon day of the month, is also believed to be the birthday of Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu. In Bengal, this festival leans more toward religion and philosophy. Early in the morning on Dol Purnima day, people dress up in white or saffron-colored clothing and wear flowers and/or garlands. Then they sing and dance to the accompaniment of traditional musical instruments such as the Veena, Dubri, Ektara, and so on. Holi here is also known as the Dol Jatra or Swing Festival. The idols are placed in a beautifully decorated palanquin and are taken around the main streets of the village or city where the celebration takes place. As the procession unfolds, the men keep spraying colored water, Gulal powder, and the fragrant Abeer at all passers-by._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: dollsofindia.com and various._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: NewsTrack

hook: Bhaithki Holi and Kadi Holi are celebrations of the festival in its musical avatar. Do you know in which Indian States Holi is celebrated with the predominance of music?
explanation: The Kumaon, Khadi, or Baithki Holi is celebrated in the Kumaon region of Uttarakhand. It commences from the temple premises. Holi singers, or Holiyars as they are popularly referred to, gather to sing Holi songs. They are based on classical ragas and are spiritual in nature with a mass appeal._x000D_ _x000D_ The Khadi Holi is celebrated in rural areas. People wear traditional white churidar kurtas during celebrations. They sing simple, rustic songs and dance to the tune of simple accompaniments such as Dhol and Hurka._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: dollsofindia.com and various._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Jagran

hook: Do you know in the Lathmaar Holi, women pretend to beat up men in mock admonishment? Where is Lathmaar Holi celebrated?
explanation: The Braj region (around Mathura-Vrindavan) is the venue for Lathmaar Holi. Krishna, who was from Nandgoan, planned to spoil Radha’s rosy complexion by smearing Gulal ( colours) on her face. He traveled to Barsana to achieve this mischievous feat, but this act makes the Gopis furious. They chased him and his accomplices back to Nandgaon with lathis. Holi is celebrated most intensely in the Braj region and it usually lasts for eight days. The Krishna-Radha episode is recreated at both Nandgaon and Barsana by the local dwellers. First, the women of Barsana pretend to beat up the men of Nandgaon with lathis. The next day it is the turn of the women of Nandgaon follow-up the ritual with Barsana men. Both Holi celebrations are held at the temple complexes amidst a riot of colours and cheerful festive music. Source: Outlook Traveller, Lasya Nadimpally Picture Credit: Avishek Das, Flickr

hook: Do you know a festival very similar to Holi is held in a revered Shiva temple in one of the Indian States? The only difference with Holi is that turmeric is used instead of colours.
explanation: The Khandoba temple in Jejuri near Pune celebrates the Bhandara festival. Officially it is the Somavati Amavasya festival, the new moon that coincides with a Monday. It doesn't have a fixed date in any calendar and can happen twice or thrice in a year. Khandoba, a manifestation of Shiva, is the deity in Jejuri. On the day of the Bhandara celebration, Khandoba and his consort Mhalsa are taken to the river for a bath and then brought back in a procession. Immense quantities of turmeric mixed with coconut powder are thrown all around, turning everything yellow. The festival is a daylong affair. Jejuri is also believed to be the location where Shivaji met his father, Shahaji, after a decade during which his father was busy with southern exploits. Turmeric is also the medium of celebration of Holi in Kerala by Konkani migrants. The festival, which is otherwise not celebrated by natives of Kerala, is called ManjalKuli. Source: Temples and Legends of Maharastra, M.S Mate Picture Credit: Zee News

hook: Holi is an integral part of Hindi Films past and present, but do you know of a film centered around a Holi like festival?
explanation: Utsav (1984), starring Shashi Kapoor, Rekha, and Shekhar Suman is based on an ancient Sanskrit play called Mrichchhakatikam (The Little Clay Cart). The temple of Kaama, the garden attached to it, and the fair and procession of the Kaama festival are the important aspects of this enchanting play that was turned into the critically acclaimed movie Utsav. This feature serves as another ancient reference to spring festivals, depicting the popular worship of Kaama, the God of Love._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: indiafacts.org, Nagaraj Paturi_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Shruti Yatam, arre.co.in

hook: Holi is generally seen as a community-centric- festival. Yet, the Holi celebrations in Mathura-Vrindavan temples are well known. Do you know which other famous temple is associated with traditional Holi celebrations?
explanation: VirupaksasVasantotsavaChampu is a text ((by Ahobala) that is set in Hampi, the capital of the Vijayanagara kingdom. It describes a nine-night-long spring festival, Vasantotsava, celebrated in honour of Virupaksa (Siva). It describes a very elaborate Rathotsava of Shiva and Parvati. The Vijayanagara King is a central feature of the worship and celebrations. It also describes in detail class dynamics and social behavior during the festival. Interesting trivia of the hunting ritual on the following day when Shiva rides alone without Parvati is also shared through this festival. Source: Vasanthotsava - Dances of the Spring Festival in Vijayanagar Times, Padmini Seshadri and Choodamani Nandagopal Vasantosava, Spring Festivals of India, Leona Anderson Picture Credit: Kolkata Museum

hook: Holi celebrations are fun for most, but some do not enjoy being smeared by colours. Do you know which historical religious figure was advised to stay away from Holi festivities in his own town?
explanation: In Buddhist literature, Balanakkhatta is mentioned as a festival similar to Holi. It is celebrated for seven days. People smear themselves with cow dung and ashes and indulge in coarse talk. They stand in doorways and insult everyone who passes by, be it relatives, friends, or monks. Those who refuse to endure the coarse talk would pay them money. In Sravasti, where Buddha lived after enlightenment, this ritual was observed, and the crowd was obnoxious. His followers advised him to stay put in the monastery along with other monks. For seven days, they sent food and escorted them to the city on the eighth day after the celebrations had ended. Source: Buddhist Legends, Volume 28, Eugene Watson Burlingame Picture Credit: We asked Dall E, the artificial intelligence engine to simulate a picture of a great man in the midst of Holi.

hook: This Hindu text has the earliest reference to Krishna. He is referred to as “Devakiputra Krishna, a disciple of Ghora Angirasa”. Which text is this?
explanation: The Chandogya Upanishad (dated to atleast 7th century BCE), has the earliest reference. It refers to Devakiputra Krishna as a disciple of Ghora Angirasa. Krishna & Angirasa are referred as Surya worshippers. Angirasa was associated with the Bhojas, related to Krishna’s clan. They practice “tapodanam, arjjvam, ahimsa, satyam.” This is similar to what the Mahabharata says. The Chandogya calls Krishna ‘Achyuta’, a popular name of Krishna. Scholars concluded that the Krishna of the Chandogya is the same as the Krishna of Mahabharata. Another early reference is by the grammarian Panini (6th cent BCE) who calls his followers ‘Vasudevakas’ and ‘Arjunakas’, after Krishna’s chief disciple Arjuna. The linguist Yaska (5th cent BCE) also mentions ‘Akrura’ who appears in the stories of Krishna’s fight with Kansa. Source: Professor Meenakshi Jain. “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura.”

hook: A relative of Krishna, he remained neutral in the Mahabharat war. In early Krishna related sculptures, he is as prominent as Krishna himself. Who is this?
explanation: Balarama is Krishna’s brother. He holds a plough, likes Duryodhana and remains neutral in the Bharata war. Heroes from the Yadava/ Vrishni clan of Krishna were also deified. Balarama was called ‘Haladar’ or ‘Samkarsna’ and was associated with farming and was popular. Krishna was worshipped in a triad with his brother Balarama and sister Ekanamsa. He was also worshipped as Panchvira (5 heroes) with his brother Balarama, sons Pradyumna & Samba and grandson Aniruddh. These were considered emanations of Vasudeva. This practice gradually got assimilated with the Vedic Vishnu, who was one of the 12 forms of Sun in the Vedas. The Wikimedia image is that of Balarama from Rajbari, West Bengal. Source: Professor Meenakshi Jain. “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura.” Picture Credit: Wikimedia

hook: In late 4th century BCE, the Greek Megasthenes identified this city as a stronghold of Krishna worship. Which city is it?
explanation: Megasthenes was the ambassador of Seleucus, Alexander, successor. He stayed at the court of Chandragupta Maurya at Pataliputra (Patna). He wrote, “The Herakles is held in special honour by the Sourasenoi who possess two large cities, Methora and Cleisobara and through whose country flows the river Iobares.”_x000D_ _x000D_ The Greeks called Krishna Herakles, after their own hero Hercules. The Surasena were a tribe associated with Yadavas. Methora, Cleisobara and Iobares have been identified as Mathura, Gokul and Yamuna._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Professor Meenakshi Jain. “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura.”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Amar Chitra Katha

hook: The army of one of the greatest kings of India (who fought Alexander) carried an image of Krishna in front, as an inspiration to soldiers. Whose army was this?
explanation: Roman historian, Quintus Curtius, in his “Histories of Alexander the Great,” recorded a report of one of the companions of Alexander. The army of Porus carried an image of ‘Hercules’ as he advanced on the Vitasta (Jhelum) river. Greeks & Romans likened Krishna to their own hero Hercules. He wrote about the image, “To desert the bearers of this image was reckoned a disgraceful military offence and they even ordained death as a penalty of those who failed to bring it back from the battlefield.” The Wikimedia image is of Alexander meeting Porus from Muller Baden, Germany. Source: Professor Meenakshi Jain. “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura.” Picture Credit: Wikimedia

hook: The Naneghat inscription is an early written reference to Krishna. It was made by Satvahanas and is close to a large modern city. Near which city is this inscription?
explanation: Satavahanas or Andhras created the first empire in the Deccan. The empire covered areas of Gujarat, Maharashtra, Andhra, Telengana & Karnataka between the 2nd cent BCE to 2nd cent CE. Their victory over the foreign origin Sakas is believed to be commemorated as the start of Saka era in 78CE. The Indian national calendar adopted in 1957, uses this era._x000D_ _x000D_ The Nanaghat inscriptions in the Western Ghats are described as, “the oldest historical documents of Western India, more important than all other cave inscriptions taken together.” They are attributed to Queen Naganika, the Naga wife of Satkarni I and dated between 200-150 BCE. The inscriptions have an invocation to Krishna, “Adoration to the two descendants of the moon, Samkarsana & Vasudeva, who are endowed with majesty.”_x000D_ _x000D_ The Wikimedia image is of Satvahana coins in the family name of Satkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Professor Meenakshi Jain. “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura.”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia

hook: The Heliodorus pillar was erected by a Greek ambassador to India to venerate Krishna. It is one of the earliest surviving Hindu religious structures in India. In which State is this pillar?
explanation: Heliodorus describes himself as a Bhagvata – worshipper of Vasudeva and that he set up the pillar in his honor. The pillar is dated to 150-100BCE. Nearby the remains of an ancient temple dated to 3rd cent BCE have been found. The pillar stands in Besnagar, near Vidisha in Madhya Pradesh. Heliodorus came to the court of the Sunga governor of the region._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Professor Meenakshi Jain. “Vasudeva Krishna and Mathura.”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia

hook: Rock carvings in this far off region attest to the early spread of Krishna worship in North West India. Balarama and Krishna are seen in these carvings. Where are they found?
